---
title: "Workshop Speaking Notes"
subtitle: "Matching and Weighting Workshop"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    code-links:
      - text: Workshop deck
        href: matching_workshop.html
      - text: Main report
        href: ../articles/matching_methods_report.html
      - text: Labs hub
        href: ../labs/index.html
      - text: Black Politicians lab
        href: ../labs/black_politicians_lab.html
      - text: NSW + CPS lab
        href: ../labs/nsw_cps_benchmark_lab.html
execute:
  echo: false
  eval: false
---

## How To Use These Notes

Use this page as the standalone facilitator script for the workshop deck.

- Keep it open beside the deck if you do not want to rely on presenter view.
- Use the run-of-show table to manage the hour.
- Use the slide-by-slide notes when you need prompts, transitions, or lab instructions.
- Keep the [main report](../articles/matching_methods_report.html) open during the worked-example segment.

## Recommended Setup

Open these pages before the session starts:

- [Workshop deck](matching_workshop.html)
- [Main report](../articles/matching_methods_report.html)
- [Labs hub](../labs/index.html)
- [Black Politicians lab](../labs/black_politicians_lab.html)
- [NSW + CPS benchmark lab](../labs/nsw_cps_benchmark_lab.html)

If you know in advance which exercise follows the talk, also open that lab's evaluated-code page.

## 60-Minute Run Of Show

| Time | Slides | Aim |
|---|---|---|
| `0-5` | Title slide, Session Promise | Set expectations and tell people this is a design session |
| `5-10` | 60-Minute Arc, The Comparison Problem | Frame why adjustment is needed in observational work |
| `10-20` | Workflow, Four Axes, Method Map | Give the shared language for the rest of the hour |
| `20-35` | Worked Example, Main Report Anchor, Main Report Teaches | Translate the `lalonde` report into a method comparison |
| `35-45` | Decision Rule, Lab Launch, What Stays Fixed | Turn the report into a repeatable lab standard |
| `45-58` | Lab slides | Tell participants what to run, what to save, and what question to answer |
| `58-60` | Handoff, Closing Line | Move the room cleanly into the practical work |

## Slide-By-Slide Notes

### Title Slide

- `Purpose:` Open with the claim that the session is about design judgement rather than software.
- `Say:` Matching and weighting matter because they make the comparison problem explicit. They do not manufacture a credible causal design on their own.
- `Prompt:` Ask what participants usually look at first in a matching write-up. If the answer is the effect estimate, use that as the problem the workshop is correcting.
- `Transition:` Move straight into the session promise so the room knows the talk ends with actionable lab handoff.

### Session Promise

- `Purpose:` Tell participants what they should be able to do by the end of the hour.
- `Say:` The goal is to explain the methods, compare their costs, and know where to start in the lab notebooks.
- `Emphasis:` Keep repeating that the job is to defend a design, not to memorize a preferred algorithm.
- `Transition:` Use the promise slide to justify the time map on the next slide.

### 60-Minute Arc

- `Purpose:` Give yourself permission to keep the session moving.
- `Say:` The first half builds the concepts, the middle translates the main report, and the final third is operational lab briefing.
- `Prompt:` Tell people that lab instructions are coming later so they do not panic if the early slides stay conceptual.
- `Transition:` Move from timing to the underlying problem: why adjustment is needed at all.

### The Comparison Problem

- `Purpose:` Explain what matching and weighting are trying to repair.
- `Say:` Observational data rarely hand us controls who are automatically comparable to the treated group.
- `Say:` Matching changes which units can be compared. Weighting changes which units carry influence. Both are design moves.
- `Warning:` State clearly that neither method rescues post-treatment covariates, unmeasured confounding, or no overlap.
- `Transition:` Once the room sees the problem, move to the workflow that disciplines the response.

### The Non-Negotiable Workflow

- `Purpose:` Establish the common sequence that all later slides and labs will reuse.
- `Say:` Name the estimand first, then the covariates, then diagnose the raw sample, then adjust the design, then estimate last.
- `Prompt:` Ask which step gets skipped most often in applied work. The answer is usually the raw-diagnostics step or the estimand statement.
- `Transition:` Move to the next slide by saying that a workflow is only useful if people know what counts as design quality.

### Read Design Quality On Four Axes

- `Purpose:` Stop the audience from equating design quality with a single balance table.
- `Say:` Balance is necessary but not sufficient. You also need overlap, treated retention, and weight diagnostics.
- `Say:` In matching, the cost often appears as dropped units. In weighting, the cost often appears as low effective sample size or extreme weights.
- `Prompt:` Ask which of the four axes tends to get underreported in their own work.
- `Transition:` Then give the method map so participants know how the main methods typically trade these costs.

### Method Map

- `Purpose:` Give a clean comparison before the worked example starts.
- `Say:` Exact matching is strongest on auditability, `CEM` is the clearest matched-sample compromise, and entropy balancing is strongest on treated retention plus observed mean balance.
- `Warning:` Do not present this as a ranking. Present it as a map of recurring tradeoffs.
- `Transition:` Move to the `lalonde` report as the place where those tradeoffs become concrete.

### Worked Example

- `Purpose:` Signal the shift from abstract method talk to the report's example.
- `Say:` The main report is a design comparison on one target, not three disconnected software demos.
- `Transition:` Use this divider to physically open the report in another tab or window if you are presenting live.

### Main Report Anchor

- `Purpose:` Tell participants exactly how to navigate the report during delivery.
- `Say:` Keep the same `ATT` in view and move through `Pre-Match Diagnostics`, then exact matching, then `CEM`, then entropy balancing, then `Comparing the Methods`.
- `Prompt:` Ask why the pre-match section matters. The answer you want is that it shows the burden the design has to carry.
- `Transition:` Then move to the summary slide that condenses the report's comparative result.

### What The Main Report Teaches

- `Purpose:` Land the report's main comparison in one place.
- `Say:` Exact matching keeps almost the full treated group but leaves some imbalance unresolved. `CEM` improves balance visibly but prunes heavily. Entropy balancing preserves the treated group while shrinking the control information through weights.
- `Emphasis:` The numbers on this slide make the cost concrete. Read them aloud.
- `Prompt:` Ask which cost they find easier to defend: explicit sample pruning or less visible weight concentration.
- `Transition:` Use that question to move into the decision rule slide.

### Decision Rule, Not Winner's Podium

- `Purpose:` Convert the report into evaluator guidance.
- `Say:` Choose the strongest design the data can defend. If a stronger quasi-experimental design is available, use that instead of defaulting to matching.
- `Say:` Within matching and weighting, choose according to the causal question, the support problem, and the cost you are willing to report.
- `Prompt:` State explicitly that a good applied answer can be "this design is not credible enough."
- `Transition:` Now that the design rule is clear, move into the lab-launch section.

### Lab Launch

- `Purpose:` Mark the shift from conceptual delivery into operational instructions.
- `Say:` The labs are not separate standards. They reuse the same workflow with different data structures and different support problems.
- `Transition:` Move immediately to the "what stays fixed" slide so the audience hears the continuity before the dataset details.

### What Stays Fixed Across Every Lab

- `Purpose:` Stabilize the room before you start naming different notebooks.
- `Say:` Every lab starts with treatment, outcome, covariates, and estimand. Every lab requires raw diagnostics before adjustment. Every lab ends with a design judgement, not just an estimate.
- `Prompt:` Tell participants they should expect to report worst imbalance, retention or `ESS`, and one defended limitation every time.
- `Transition:` Use this slide to justify the lab-overview table.

### Lab Bundle At A Glance

- `Purpose:` Help participants pick the right exercise without opening every file blindly.
- `Say:` Black Politicians is the default first breakout. NSW + CPS adds a benchmark question. HISP is the escalation lab. Titanic is the shortest recap.
- `Prompt:` If you are teaching live, tell the room which one you are actually assigning.
- `Transition:` Move to the hand-back slide so participants know what evidence they need from any lab they choose.

### What Every Group Should Hand Back

- `Purpose:` Define success before they start coding.
- `Say:` The deliverable is a short design memo in spoken form: target, covariates, best and worst diagnostics, cost paid, preferred design, remaining limitation.
- `Emphasis:` This is the slide to use if discussion later drifts into unstructured code-watching.
- `Transition:` Move into the specific exercises and keep each briefing tight.

### Exercise 1: Black Politicians

- `Purpose:` Launch the first and most direct follow-on lab.
- `Say:` This is the closest extension of the main report because it compares exact matching, `CEM`, and entropy balancing on a fresh observational problem.
- `Run:` Work from `Step 1` to `Step 7` in order. Do not skip the raw comparison or the baseline-balance benchmark.
- `Capture:` Ask groups to save `raw_summary`, the pre-adjustment diagnostic, matched counts, `ebal_weight_summary`, and the completed comparison table.
- `Prompt:` Ask which design they would defend to a policy audience and what cost they would have to admit.
- `Transition:` Move to NSW + CPS by saying the next lab adds an external benchmark rather than just a method comparison.

### Exercise 2: NSW + CPS Benchmark

- `Purpose:` Brief the benchmark-recovery exercise.
- `Say:` This lab matters because the design is judged not only on balance but also on how close it gets to the experimental NSW estimate.
- `Run:` Go from `Step 1` to `Step 8`, with real time spent on the benchmark, overlap diagnostics, and the final comparison table.
- `Capture:` Tell groups to keep `benchmark_summary`, `overlap_summary`, matched counts, `ebal_weight_summary`, and `estimate_comparison`.
- `Prompt:` Ask which method gets closest to the benchmark without making the weighted or matched design too fragile.
- `Transition:` Move to HISP by saying the next lab changes the teaching question from matching performance to design escalation.

### Exercise 3: HISP IE in Practice

- `Purpose:` Explain why this notebook is structurally different from the other labs.
- `Say:` This is not a pure matching notebook. It follows the do-file logic across weaker and stronger designs so participants can see when matching should stop being the default.
- `Run:` Use `Step 1` first, then skim `Step 2` and `Step 3` as weak contrasts, spend more time on `Step 4` to `Step 9`, and leave `Step 10` for overflow.
- `Capture:` The core outputs are the effect movement across methods, the randomized-branch balance checks, the matching-branch diagnostics, and one sentence on whether matching adds anything once `IV`, `RDD`, or `DiD` are available.
- `Prompt:` Ask what this notebook teaches about escalation inside the quasi-experimental toolkit.
- `Transition:` Move to Titanic as the shortest visual recap.

### Exercise 4: Titanic

- `Purpose:` Brief the shortest and most visible support-focused lab.
- `Say:` Titanic is useful because the support cells are visible, exact matching is feasible, and subclassification can be compared on one page.
- `Run:` Work through `Support Cells Before Matching`, `Exact Matching`, `Propensity-Score Subclassification`, and `Comparison Of Estimates`.
- `Capture:` Ask for the support-cell table, balance comparison, subclass counts, and the final estimate table.
- `Prompt:` Ask what the Titanic example makes easier to see than the higher-dimensional labs.
- `Transition:` Move to the handoff slide and tell the room exactly which pages to open next.

### Handoff

- `Purpose:` End the talk with a clean transition rather than one more conceptual point.
- `Say:` Open the main report, the notes page, and the assigned lab before moving into coding or discussion.
- `Say:` If you are facilitating, this is the moment to assign groups or name the first notebook explicitly.
- `Transition:` Use the closing line as the final standard the room should carry into the exercises.

### Closing Line

- `Purpose:` Leave the room with one sentence that ties the whole workshop together.
- `Say:` Use the strongest design the data can defend, then report the cost of that design plainly.
- `Optional close:` Ask one participant to restate that sentence in their own words before the breakout starts.

## Minimal Version If Time Slips

If you lose `10-15` minutes, keep only this sequence:

1. `Session Promise`
2. `The Non-Negotiable Workflow`
3. `Method Map`
4. `What The Main Report Teaches`
5. `Decision Rule, Not Winner's Podium`
6. `Exercise 1: Black Politicians`
7. `Handoff`

Cut first:

- the detailed lab-overview table
- the HISP and Titanic briefings
- repeated prompts to the room

## Final Facilitator Check

Before you leave the notes page, make sure the room can answer all three:

1. What estimand are we targeting?
2. What design cost did we pay to improve comparability?
3. What claim is still out of reach even after adjustment?
